title: Ceratochloa sitchensis
taxonomy: Ceratochloa sitchensis is classified as a species of grass in the family Poaceae. This classification is further grouped under the subfamily Chloridoideae and tribe Cynodonteae. As a member of the genus Ceratochloa, it shares characteristics with other related species. Ceratochloa sitchensis is native to the Pacific Northwest region of North America. Its classification is an important aspect in understanding the plant's evolutionary relationships and ecological roles.

benefits: Ceratochloa sitchensis, also known as sea barley grass or sea bluegrass, has been traditionally used for medicinal purposes in some regions. intro:
There is no information available on the plant Ceratochloa sitchensis. However, I found a similar plant called Ceratochloa (now known as Elymus) sitchensis, commonly known as Sitka wildrye or Sitka meadow grass. 

Ceratochloa sitchensis, also known as Elymus sitchensis, is a perennial grass native to the Pacific Northwest region of North America. It has been reported to have potential health benefits, although more research is needed to confirm its efficacy. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help in reducing pain and swelling. 

The bioactive constituents of Elymus sitchensis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins. These compounds may be responsible for the plant's reported health benefits. 

Elymus sitchensis is not commonly used in herbal medicine, but its leaves and stems can be made into teas, infusions, or tinctures. However, there is limited information available on its traditional uses or commercial herbal preparations.
